title: Topic 105: The Decoy Effect in Game Pricing: How Bad Deals Make Other Deals Look Good
content:
### ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the shadowy world of video game pricing, where developers, armed with a psychological cheat code known as the “decoy effect,” manipulate players into spending more than they intended. We explore how the strategic placement of a seemingly terrible deal can make a slightly less terrible, but more expensive, deal look like the bargain of the century. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of in-game stores and premium currency packages, we reveal the subtle art of making gamers feel smart about their poor financial decisions. This research serves as a crucial reminder that in the grand game of consumerism, the house always wins, especially when it designed the board, the pieces, and the irresistible, asymmetrically dominated third option.

### INTRODUCTION

Welcome, dear reader, to the carnival of capitalism that is the modern video game marketplace. A place where logic takes a backseat to impulse, and the line between a good deal and a grand deception is blurred by a psychological sleight of hand known as the decoy effect. Have you ever found yourself staring at a screen, faced with a choice between a “Small Pouch of Gleaming Gems” for $4.99 and a “Large Chest of Radiant Jewels” for $19.99, only to find your eyes drawn to the “Medium Sack of Shiny Stones” for a suspiciously specific $18.99? If you’ve ever felt a strange compulsion to purchase the Large Chest, feeling like you’ve just outsmarted the system, then you, my friend, have been successfully duped by the decoy effect. It’s the art of making a bad deal look so terrible that another, slightly less bad deal, appears to be the most rational choice a human being could possibly make. This paper will embark on a journey into the heart of this phenomenon, exploring how game developers and publishers have mastered the art of psychological pricing to gently, almost lovingly, guide our wallets open. We will dissect the mechanics of this cognitive bias, laugh at our own gullibility, and perhaps, just perhaps, emerge from this academic rabbit hole a little wiser and a lot more suspicious of any deal that seems too good to be true, especially when it’s sitting next to one that’s laughably bad.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

The decoy effect, or as it’s known in more formal, less fun circles, the “asymmetric dominance effect,” is not a new trick. It was first dragged into the harsh light of academic scrutiny by Huber, Payne, and Puto in 1982 [1]. These pioneers of psychological chicanery discovered that, contrary to the prevailing economic theories of the time which held that humans were rational beings who made logical choices, we are, in fact, easily befuddled creatures of habit and comparison. Their work was a direct challenge to the “regularity principle,” a rather quaint notion that adding a new option to a set of choices cannot possibly increase the popularity of one of the original options. Huber and his colleagues, presumably after witnessing someone buy a comically overpriced medium soda, decided to test this. They found that by introducing a third, strategically terrible option (the decoy), they could nudge people towards a specific, more expensive choice (the target), as if guiding them by an invisible, money-seeking hand.

No discussion of the decoy effect would be complete without genuflecting at the altar of Dan Ariely, the high priest of behavioral economics. Ariely’s now-famous experiment with *The Economist* magazine’s subscription options is the stuff of legend [2]. He presented his students with three choices: an online-only subscription for $59, a print-only subscription for $125, and a print-and-online subscription for $125. The print-only option was, of course, the decoy, a deal so obviously inferior to the print-and-online package that it might as well have come with a free, complimentary kick in the shins. A whopping 84% of his students chose the combo deal, feeling like they had just pulled off the heist of the century. But when Ariely removed the decoy, the print-only option, the majority of students suddenly found the $59 online-only subscription to be perfectly adequate. The decoy had worked its magic, making a $125 purchase feel like a bargain by comparison. This simple, elegant experiment exposed the irrationality of our decision-making and provided a blueprint for marketers everywhere to exploit our cognitive shortcomings with gleeful abandon.

### ANALYSIS

So, how does this psychological voodoo work? Why do our brains, the same organs that can contemplate the vastness of the cosmos and appreciate the subtle nuances of a well-crafted meme, so consistently fall for this cheap parlor trick? The answer lies in a cocktail of cognitive biases, each one a delightful little quirk in our mental wiring that makes us susceptible to the siren song of the decoy. At its core, the decoy effect is a masterclass in exploiting our inherent laziness. We are, by nature, cognitive misers. We don’t like to think too hard if we can avoid it, especially when it comes to the mundane act of buying things. The decoy effect simplifies the decision-making process by providing a clear, albeit illusory, winner. When faced with two options that are difficult to compare, our brains tend to short-circuit. But introduce a third, asymmetrically dominated option, and suddenly, the choice becomes a no-brainer. The decoy provides a convenient point of comparison, a yardstick against which one of the other options suddenly looks like a superstar.

This is where the concept of “asymmetric dominance” comes into play. The decoy is designed to be clearly inferior to the “target” option in every respect, but only partially inferior to the “competitor” option. This creates a lopsided comparison that makes the target shine. It’s like being asked to choose between a date with a supermodel, a date with a regular person, and a date with a supermodel who also happens to be a billionaire philanthropist. The regular person never stood a chance. In the world of game pricing, this translates to a premium currency pack that offers slightly more currency for a much higher price (the competitor), a pack that offers a lot more currency for a slightly higher price (the target), and a pack that offers a middling amount of currency for a price that is almost the same as the target (the decoy). The decoy is the ugly friend that makes the target look like a prom queen.

But it’s not just about making the choice easier. The decoy effect also taps into our deep-seated fear of making the “wrong” decision. This is known as “loss aversion,” the principle that the pain of losing something is psychologically about twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining something of equal value. The decoy effect frames the choice in a way that makes us feel like we’re avoiding a loss. By choosing the target, we’re not just getting a good deal; we’re actively avoiding the “bad” deal that is the decoy. We get a little dopamine hit from our perceived cleverness, a self-congratulatory pat on the back for outsmarting the system, all while the game developers are laughing all the way to the bank.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The gaming industry, a veritable petri dish of psychological manipulation, is rife with examples of the decoy effect in action. Take, for instance, the ubiquitous premium currency bundles found in most free-to-play mobile games. A player might be presented with the following options: 100 “Dragon Crystals” for $0.99, 550 Dragon Crystals for $4.99, or 1200 Dragon Crystals for $9.99. The 550-crystal bundle is the decoy. It’s a better deal than the 100-crystal bundle, but it pales in comparison to the 1200-crystal bundle, which offers more than double the crystals for only double the price. The decoy exists solely to make the $9.99 bundle look like an incredible bargain, nudging players who might have only spent a dollar, or nothing at all, to splurge on the most expensive option.

But the decoy effect isn’t just limited to premium currencies. It can be found in the pricing of cosmetic items, expansion packs, and even subscription models. A game might offer a “Standard Edition” for $59.99, a “Deluxe Edition” with a few extra digital goodies for $79.99, and a “Collector’s Edition” with a physical statue and an art book for a whopping $199.99. The Deluxe Edition, in this case, can act as a decoy, making the Standard Edition seem like a more reasonable purchase for those who aren’t interested in the physical collectibles. It can also, conversely, make the Collector’s Edition seem more appealing to those who were on the fence, as the perceived value of the physical items is inflated by the relatively small price jump from the Deluxe Edition.

One of the most insidious examples of the decoy effect can be found in the “battle pass” systems that have become so popular in recent years. A player might be offered a free battle pass with limited rewards, a “premium” battle pass with more rewards for $9.99, and a “premium plus” battle pass that includes the premium rewards and instantly unlocks 25 tiers for $24.99. The premium plus option is the decoy. It’s a terrible value proposition for most players, as the 25 tiers can be easily unlocked through regular gameplay. However, its presence makes the $9.99 premium battle pass seem like an absolute steal, a small price to pay for a wealth of exclusive content. The decoy has done its job, turning a “free” game into a recurring subscription for millions of players.

### DISCUSSION

The rampant use of the decoy effect in game pricing is more than just a clever marketing tactic; it’s a window into the very soul of human irrationality. It reveals that our decisions are not always our own, but are instead shaped by the subtle, often invisible, forces of our environment. We are not the rational, utility-maximizing beings that classical economics would have us believe. We are, in fact, a bundle of cognitive biases, heuristics, and emotional responses, all of which can be easily manipulated by a well-placed decoy. This has profound implications for our understanding of consumer behavior. It suggests that we are not always in control of our purchasing decisions, and that our perception of value is highly malleable. We are not looking for the “best” deal in an absolute sense, but rather the “best” deal relative to the other options presented to us. This is a crucial distinction, and one that game developers have exploited with ruthless efficiency.

Furthermore, the decoy effect highlights our deep-seated need to feel smart and in control. When we choose the target option, we feel like we’ve outsmarted the system, that we’ve found a loophole in the pricing matrix. This feeling of satisfaction, this little ego boost, is a powerful motivator. It’s a form of self-deception, a way of convincing ourselves that we are savvy consumers who are immune to the wiles of marketers. But in reality, we are simply playing the part that has been assigned to us, following the breadcrumbs that have been laid out for us by the game’s designers. The decoy effect, then, is not just a tool for increasing profits; it’s a tool for managing the player’s ego, for making them feel good about spending money. It’s a masterclass in the art of making people happy to be fleeced.

### CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the decoy effect is the secret sauce that makes the multi-billion dollar gaming industry go ‘round. It’s the invisible hand that guides us, with a gentle yet firm grip, towards the more expensive option, all while whispering sweet nothings about value and savings in our ears. We have seen how this psychological phenomenon, born from the hallowed halls of academia, has been weaponized by game developers to create a veritable minefield of cognitive traps, each one designed to separate us from our hard-earned cash. From the humble beginnings of premium currency bundles to the sophisticated architecture of battle pass systems, the decoy effect is everywhere, a constant reminder that our brains are, for all their wondrous complexity, remarkably easy to fool. So, the next time you find yourself staring at a screen, faced with a choice that seems too good to be true, remember the decoy. And then, in all likelihood, buy the expensive option anyway. Because, as we’ve learned, feeling smart is often more important than being smart, especially when there’s a shiny new virtual hat on the line.
references:
[1] Huber, J., Payne, J. W., & Puto, C. (1982). Adding asymmetrically dominated alternatives: Violations of regularity and the similarity hypothesis. *Journal of Consumer Research*, 9(1), 90–98.

[2] Ariely, D. (2008). *Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions*. HarperCollins.
